articles.title: How Did People Learn About the Declaration of Independence in 1776?
articles.summary: Imagine no internet, no TV, no phones. How would you find out that your country just declared independence? That's what happened in 1776. A new book by historian Emily Sneff tells the story. The Declaration was printed on big sheets called broadsides and read aloud in town squares. Some people were thrilled, others were worried. The first printed copies didn't even have the signers' names. It took months for the news to reach everyone. This story shows how different news was 250 years ago.

articles.title: Tennis Star Carlos Alcaraz Will Miss the French Open Due to Injury
articles.summary: Bad news for tennis fans! Carlos Alcaraz, the two-time defending champion, won't play in the French Open next month. He hurt his right wrist at the Barcelona Open and tests showed he needs to rest. Alcaraz is only 22 but has already won seven Grand Slam titles. He won the Australian Open this year. His rival Jannik Sinner said he was sad to hear the news and hopes Alcaraz will be back for Wimbledon. This opens the door for Sinner to win his first French Open.

articles.title: FDA Approves First Gene Therapy for Inherited Deafness
articles.summary: Big news in medicine! The FDA has approved the first gene therapy for a type of inherited deafness. The therapy, called Otarmeni, helps people who are born deaf because of a problem in a gene called OTOF. It uses a harmless virus to deliver a working copy of the gene into the ear. In a trial, 16 out of 20 people improved their hearing within six months. Some could even hear whispers. The company making the therapy says the treatment itself will be free for patients in the U.S. This is a huge step forward for gene therapy.
